bins.name: Voltage-Based Threshold Flexibility
bins.desc: SPP opposes a single federal MW threshold and argues that what counts as "large" depends on the voltage level of interconnection and local system conditions. SPP's own tariff uses 10 MW at or below 69 kV and 50 MW above 69 kV. SPP wants final rules to let each transmission provider set its own thresholds.
quotes: SPP requests that any final rules in this matter do not include any specific MW threshold, but instead, the final rules should allow each transmission provider to propose thresholds specific to the needs of their transmission systems.

SPP proposed the following threshold to qualify as a HILL: "(1) 10 MW or more if connected to the Transmission System at a voltage level less than or equal to 69 kV; or (2) 50 MW or more if connected to the Transmission System at a voltage level greater than 69 kV."

bins.name: Full-Load Study for Hybrid Facilities
bins.desc: SPP argues that hybrid facilities must be studied on their full gross withdrawal and injection capacity, not net rights. A generator trip can expose the full load to the grid instantly, and studying only net rights would leave SPP unable to plan for that contingency. Coincident-peak billing on net rights also risks shifting costs to other consumers.
quotes: system protection facilities may not fully protect the grid from stability issues, and as such, the full load at a hybrid facility should be considered when applying a MW threshold.

SPP cannot appropriately study the actual impacts of that load on the Transmission System without studying the full impact of its potential withdrawals from the Transmission System. Although this load may have a generator capable of offsetting the load at most times, in the event that generator goes offline, SPP would have to provide the full MW amount to serve that load, and would, therefore, experience the effects of its full withdrawal even if only temporarily.

Even with system protections in place, very rapid withdrawals of large amounts of power can adversely affect the transmission grid (e.g., through rapid frequency excursions and unintended protection operations). All generators are susceptible to unexpected tripping, and protection schemes themselves may fail under fast dynamic conditions.

If the Commission mandates a transmission cost netting paradigm, which is implied by the hybrid example, consideration should also be given to the converse scenario, where a facility with 600 MW of load and 500 MW of generation has 100 MW of withdrawal rights. In this case, billing for transmission based upon coincident peak usage can be particularly problematic if the hybrid resource requires transmission to be built and maintained, yet the hybrid facility avoids the coincident peak upon which billing is currently calculated.

bins.name: Financial Commitment to Deter Speculation
bins.desc: SPP supports requiring meaningful study deposits and application fees so that only serious interconnection applicants enter the queue. SPP already applies this approach to its HILL and HILLGA processes.
quotes: SPP believes that any final rule related to the interconnection of large loads and supporting generation should include a level of financial commitment that ensures that SPP, and other transmission providers, are only studying those projects that fully intend to interconnect to their transmission system.

bins.name: Grid Protection from Full-Load Contingencies
bins.desc: SPP warns that protection systems cannot fully guard against the fast dynamic effects of a large generator trip at a hybrid facility, including rapid frequency excursions and relay misoperations. Full-load study is required to ensure protection coordination.
quotes: system protection facilities may not fully protect the grid from stability issues, and as such, the full load at a hybrid facility should be considered when applying a MW threshold.

Even with system protections in place, very rapid withdrawals of large amounts of power can adversely affect the transmission grid (e.g., through rapid frequency excursions and unintended protection operations). All generators are susceptible to unexpected tripping, and protection schemes themselves may fail under fast dynamic conditions.

bins.name: 90-Day Minimum Study Timeline
bins.desc: SPP rejects a 60-day expedited study deadline as insufficient for the transient stability, short-circuit ratio, and critical clearing time analyses that large loads require. SPP's HILL process uses 90 days and SPP views that as the practical floor.
quotes: Based on SPP's experience, 60 days is not sufficient time to study the reliability impacts of large loads. SPP's new HILL policy in the High Impact Large Load Filing provides for a 90 day study time to allow for the necessary stability studies, such as transient stability analysis, short-circuit ratio analysis, and a critical clearing time screening to determine if further electromagnetic transient stability (EMS) analysis is needed.

bins.name: Cost Allocation Aligned with SPP Tariff
bins.desc: SPP supports requiring large load customers to bear 100% of assigned upgrade costs but asks that any federal crediting mechanism align with its existing ILTCR framework under Attachment Z2 and Attachment J. SPP also asks that cost allocation decisions respect the SPP Regional State Committee's established governance role.
quotes: If the Commission determines in a final rule that large load customers should only be charged for transmission service based on their withdrawal rights, SPP urges the Commission to consider all costs incurred by granting this type of transmission service to ensure that costs are allocated appropriately so that transmission owners are held harmless by the usage.

SPP requests that any final rule adopted based on this Proposed ANOPR provide the RSC with the opportunity to provide significant input into how network upgrade costs will be allocated within SPP as it relates to the interconnection of large loads.
SPP requests that any crediting mechanism allowed under any proposed rule in this docket be consistent with Attachment Z2 and Attachment J of the SPP Tariff.

bins.name: State Law Limits on Option to Build
bins.desc: SPP cautions that an option-to-build right for large load customers may conflict with state laws restricting who may own transmission. SPP's tariff allows only eligible transmission customers to be assigned network upgrades.
quotes: the Commission should be mindful of state laws that restrict who can build and own transmission. Network Upgrades assigned through SPP's transmission service process can only be assigned to entities that request transmission service.

bins.name: Thorough Study Before Interconnection
bins.desc: SPP supports rigorous interconnection study requirements including transient stability analysis, short-circuit ratio analysis, and critical clearing time screening. SPP argues study scope must cover full gross withdrawal and injection, not net figures, and that cluster studies may be needed at high queue volumes.
quotes: SPP cannot appropriately study the actual impacts of that load on the Transmission System without studying the full impact of its potential withdrawals from the Transmission System. Although this load may have a generator capable of offsetting the load at most times, in the event that generator goes offline, SPP would have to provide the full MW amount to serve that load, and would, therefore, experience the effects of its full withdrawal even if only temporarily.

Based on SPP's experience, 60 days is not sufficient time to study the reliability impacts of large loads. SPP's new HILL policy in the High Impact Large Load Filing provides for a 90 day study time to allow for the necessary stability studies, such as transient stability analysis, short-circuit ratio analysis, and a critical clearing time screening to determine if further electromagnetic transient stability (EMS) analysis is needed.
SPP suggests allowing each region to determine the best approach to minimize the time of study.

bins.name: No Cost Shift to Existing Ratepayers
bins.desc: SPP wants transmission owners held harmless under any new large load cost regime. Usage-based or withdrawal-right-only billing must account for the full cost of transmission built and maintained for large load, and hybrid facility billing must not let customers avoid the coincident peak that drives transmission investment.
quotes: If the Commission mandates a transmission cost netting paradigm, which is implied by the hybrid example, consideration should also be given to the converse scenario, where a facility with 600 MW of load and 500 MW of generation has 100 MW of withdrawal rights. In this case, billing for transmission based upon coincident peak usage can be particularly problematic if the hybrid resource requires transmission to be built and maintained, yet the hybrid facility avoids the coincident peak upon which billing is currently calculated.

If the Commission determines in a final rule that large load customers should only be charged for transmission service based on their withdrawal rights, SPP urges the Commission to consider all costs incurred by granting this type of transmission service to ensure that costs are allocated appropriately so that transmission owners are held harmless by the usage.

SPP requests that any final rule adopted based on this Proposed ANOPR provide the RSC with the opportunity to provide significant input into how network upgrade costs will be allocated within SPP as it relates to the interconnection of large loads.

bins.name: Regional Flexibility Over Uniform Rules
bins.desc: SPP's central ask is that FERC allow each RTO/ISO and its stakeholders to design their own large load interconnection rules rather than impose a single national standard. This applies to thresholds, study timelines, study methods, and curtailment priority.
quotes: SPP encourages the Commission to provide flexibility in any rules adopted based on the vast regional differences across the country. Rather than pursuing a "one-size fits all" approach, the Commission should consider allowing each Regional Transmission Organization ("RTO") and Independent System Operator ("ISO") ("RTO/ISO") and its stakeholders the latitude to design for these differences.

SPP requests that any final rules in this matter do not include any specific MW threshold, but instead, the final rules should allow each transmission provider to propose thresholds specific to the needs of their transmission systems.

Any load connected to the SPP Transmission System, including load that agrees to be curtailed, will require energy to be produced and will take some capacity from the Transmission System; otherwise, they would not need to connect to the grid. Even when agreeing to curtail, a curtailable, or non-firm load, will impact existing and future firm load and should not be prioritized over a load that is willing to bring, or already has Designated Resources.
SPP suggests allowing each region to determine the best approach to minimize the time of study.

bins.name: SPP Regional Context
bins.desc: SPP describes its existing and in-development processes for large load interconnection: the approved HILL and HILLGA processes, the developing CHILLS conditional non-firm service, and the PALS price-adaptive load service. SPP points to 69 kV line prevalence in its eastern footprint and multi-state jurisdictional complexity as reasons regional flexibility matters.
quotes: SPP encourages the Commission to provide flexibility in any rules adopted based on the vast regional differences across the country. Rather than pursuing a "one-size fits all" approach, the Commission should consider allowing each Regional Transmission Organization ("RTO") and Independent System Operator ("ISO") ("RTO/ISO") and its stakeholders the latitude to design for these differences.

SPP proposed the following threshold to qualify as a HILL: "(1) 10 MW or more if connected to the Transmission System at a voltage level less than or equal to 69 kV; or (2) 50 MW or more if connected to the Transmission System at a voltage level greater than 69 kV."

bins.name: State Roles in Governance and Ownership
bins.desc: SPP raises two state-authority concerns: the SPP Regional State Committee's bylaw-assigned role in cost allocation must be preserved in any final rule, and state laws restricting who may build or own transmission may limit the option-to-build right for load customers.
quotes: SPP requests that any final rule adopted based on this Proposed ANOPR provide the RSC with the opportunity to provide significant input into how network upgrade costs will be allocated within SPP as it relates to the interconnection of large loads.

the Commission should be mindful of state laws that restrict who can build and own transmission. Network Upgrades assigned through SPP's transmission service process can only be assigned to entities that request transmission service.

bins.name: Alignment with Existing Tariff Provisions
bins.desc: SPP requests that any federal crediting mechanism for upgrade costs be consistent with its existing Attachment Z2 and Attachment J provisions, which provide Incremental Long Term Congestion Rights over a 10-to-20-year term.
quotes: SPP requests that any crediting mechanism allowed under any proposed rule in this docket be consistent with Attachment Z2 and Attachment J of the SPP Tariff.

bins.name: Generation Adequacy Before Load Interconnection
bins.desc: SPP argues that any new load, including curtailable load, requires corresponding generation capacity and energy. SPP warns against prioritizing non-firm load over load backed by Designated Resources, and references NERC's energy assurance standards work.
quotes: Any load connected to the SPP Transmission System, including load that agrees to be curtailed, will require energy to be produced and will take some capacity from the Transmission System; otherwise, they would not need to connect to the grid. Even when agreeing to curtail, a curtailable, or non-firm load, will impact existing and future firm load and should not be prioritized over a load that is willing to bring, or already has Designated Resources.
